title: Saddle-node bifurcation in interfacial morphology selects battery degradation phase
abstract: We propose a minimal nonlinear closure ODE for the dynamic active-area factor of a battery interface and show that it exhibits a saddle-node bifurcation when the smoothing rate saturates with surface roughness. The closure is the simplest physically motivated extension of a recently introduced single-fixed-point closure [C. Bae, in preparation (2026)]: u = K - u/(1 + alphau^2), where u = xi - 1 is the dimensionless excess active area, K the dimensionless drive, and alpha a single saturation parameter. The bifurcation occurs at K_c = 1/(2sqrt(alpha)), separating a smooth passivating phase from a morphologically unstable phase. Mapping four canonical anode configurations -- graphite, silicon composite, lithium metal, and anode-free Li/Cu -- onto the closure via end-of-cycling steady-state xi extracted from publicly available long-cycle data populates the stable branch with monotonically increasing K/K_c ratios: graphite (~0.01), silicon composite (~0.24), lithium metal (~0.73), and anode-free (~0.95). The anode-free configuration sits within 5% of the saddle-node threshold, predicting a vanishingly small operational stability window in current density, temperature, and electrolyte composition. We test three falsifiable predictions of the framework -- a critical current density, a critical temperature shift, and a mean-field critical-slowing-down exponent -- and find them broadly consistent with publicly available data. We argue that this near-critical position is universal to nucleation-controlled deposition on non-passivating substrates.
summary: The paper introduces a simple mathematical model to describe how battery interfaces transition from stable to unstable morphologies. By applying this model to different battery types, it reveals that anode-free lithium technology operates very close to a critical point of failure, suggesting a very narrow window for safe operation.
main_problem: The paper aims to model the morphological stability of battery interfaces and predict the transition between smooth passivating phases and morphologically unstable phases during degradation.
main_result: The authors identify a saddle-node bifurcation in a minimal nonlinear ODE that governs the active-area factor, predicting that anode-free Li/Cu configurations sit dangerously close to a critical threshold of instability.
method: The study uses a minimal nonlinear closure ODE approach and maps experimental steady-state data from various anode configurations onto the theoretical bifurcation framework.
model_or_system: A nonlinear closure ODE for the dimensionless excess active area (u), representing the dynamic active-area factor of battery interfaces including graphite, silicon composite, lithium metal, and anode-free Li/Cu.
key_observables: Dimensionless excess active area (u), dimensionless drive (K), and the ratio K/K_c.
important_parameters: Saturation parameter (alpha), critical drive (K_c), current density, temperature, and electrolyte composition.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the interface dynamics can be captured by a minimal nonlinear closure ODE and that the bifurcation is driven by the saturation of the smoothing rate with surface roughness.
paper_structure: The paper proposes a nonlinear ODE model, demonstrates the existence of a saddle-node bifurcation, applies the model to various battery anode configurations using public data, and tests falsifiable predictions regarding critical current density and temperature.
why_interesting: The paper applies the concept of a saddle-node bifurcation and mean-field critical slowing down to a non-equilibrium morphological transition, which is relevant to researchers studying phase transitions and stability in driven-dissipative systems.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
